title: Banks, Low Interest Rates, and Monetary Policy Transmission
abstract: I study how the secular decline in interest rates affects banks' intermediation spreads and credit supply. Following a permanent decrease in rates, bank lending may rise initially but contracts in the long run. As lower rates compress deposit spreads even well above the zero lower bound, banks' retained earnings, equity, and lending fall until loan spreads have risen enough to offset the reduction in deposit spreads. A higher inflation target can support bank lending at the cost of higher liquidity premia. I find support for the model's predictions in U.S. aggregate and bank-level data.

article.introduction:
This paper argues that thesecular decline in nominal interest rates tightens the long-run supply of bank credit, far before interest rates reach negative territory. Short-run and long-run effects differ: a decrease in interest rates stimulates lending initially, but if interest rates remain low for long, for instance, due to a decrease in the steady-state real rater∗$r^{*}$, then lending contracts in the long run.
The central mechanism is that lower interest rates compress the deposit spreads earned by banks, due to the competition between money and deposits, which takes place even at positive rates. This implies that contractionary lending effects can appear well before reaching negative rate territory: the threshold interest rate below which lower rates hurt lending is not 0%, but rather the positive nominal ratei¯$\bar{i}$below which banks cannot earn enough income from deposit spreads to sustain their net payout rate. Relative to the literature on negative policy rates motivated by European examples, this paper makes three main contributions: studying the consequences of the secular decline inr∗$r^{*}$caused by fundamental shifts in savings rates and productivity growth, instead of extreme monetary policy accommodation around a fixedr∗$r^{*}$; highlighting that bank lending can already be harmed when interest rates are positive but low, as in the United States for example; and contrasting short-run and long-run effects of lower interest rates.
I develop a tractable model that allows one to study the consequences of low interest rates through clear analytical results. The unifying mechanism is that nominal interest rates affect thecompositionof bank income.1Banks earn the sum of two spreads, reflecting activities on the two sides of their balance sheet. On the asset side, banks earn loan spreads—the difference between loan and bond rates not explained by maturity, credit risk, and operating costs. On the liability side, banks earn deposit spreads—the difference between bond and deposit rates. Both spreads can persist due to limits to arbitrage in the form of financial constraints.
The level of nominal rates matters for the composition of bank income, because savers can substitute between private liquidity (deposits) and public liquidity (money, also known as cash or currency). Since the nominal rate is the opportunity cost of money relative to bonds, that is, the effective price of holding public liquidity, a lower nominal rate makes money more attractive relative to deposits. In equilibrium, the deposit spread, which is the effective price of the competing private liquidity, must also fall. Lower nominal rates therefore reduce not only public seigniorage, but also the “private seigniorage” earned by banks from liquidity creation.
The reduction in deposit spreads does not necessarily matter for lending. If bank lending capacity remains high enough relative to loan demand—theunconstrained lending regime—there is a decoupling: the decline in private seigniorage from deposits hurts bank profitability but has no consequences on the loan market. If, however, bank lending capacity becomes too low due to low equity and/or tight leverage constraints—theconstrained lending regime—then deposit and loan markets become entangled: lower deposit profits reduce bank equity and loan supply, and loan spreads must rise in equilibrium to reflect the tighter credit conditions.
I show that in the long run, the economy is in the constrained lending regime if and only if the steady-state nominal ratei$i$is below some positive thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$. The threshold nominal ratei¯$\bar{i}$can be quite high: in the baseline calibration it is around 8%. I further show how it is affected by supply- and demand-side factors: it increases with financial frictions that tighten lending capacity (i.e., lower asset pledgeability and a higher cost of issuing equity), as well as with new technologies that decrease households' demand for liquidity, which hurts banks' deposit business.
At high nominal ratesi>i¯$i>\bar{i}$, the deposit spread is large enough to sustain banks' required return on equity (determined by banks' entry and exit dynamics and their costs of issuing new equity) without the need for a loan spread. Effectively, depositors cross-subsidize borrowers. However, ifi$i$falls belowi¯$\bar{i}$, the deposit spread must also fall, weakening this cross-subsidization. In partial equilibrium, banks' retained earnings decline, which depletes their equity and tightens their lending capacity. In general equilibrium, a loan spread must open up to clear the credit market given the reduced credit supply. The process stops only once the loan spread is high enough for bank shareholders to obtain their required return on equity in spite of the lower deposit spread. Equilibrium bank net income is stable, because the higher loan spread approximately offsets the lower deposit spread.
Importantly, following a permanent shock toi$i$, transitional dynamics to the new steady state can be nonmonotonic, in the sense that short-run and long-run effects differ: bank lending may initially rise in response to the permanent decrease ini$i$thanks to a revaluation of banks' long-term assets, before eventually declining to its permanently lower level. Maturity mismatch is key, as I show that lending can never rise initially if banks only hold short-term assets.
The role of money means that it is thenominalratei$i$that affects lending. A natural interpretation of a loweri$i$is that the “natural” real rater∗$r^{*}$declines, holding inflation fixed. Buti$i$also depends on the central bank's inflation targetπ$\pi$through the Fisher equationi≈r∗+π$i\approx r^{*}+\pi$. The strong link between deposit and loan spreads has normative implications for the optimal long-run inflation target, which calls for a departure from the traditional Friedman rule that seeks to minimize the cost of holding liquidity. Setting a low inflation target and thus a low nominal rate has the standard benefit of reducing the cost of liquidity, but has a welfare cost for borrowers who must face higher loan spreads.
I present aggregate and cross-sectional facts on the effect of long-run interest changes on U.S. banks consistent with the predictions of the model. I find support for long-run contractionary effects of low rates on lending. Over the past 20 years, the maturity-adjusted aggregate spread between the yield earned by U.S. banks on loans and Treasuries has doubled, despite no apparent upward trend in credit risk and a clear downward trend in operating costs. Meanwhile, the total spread between the yield earned by banks on loans and the yield they pay on deposits, that is, the sum of loan and deposit spreads, has remained extremely stable since 1955. But as interest rates have declined, the composition of the sum has shifted toward lower deposit spreads and higher loan spreads, exactly as in the model.
Aggregate trends are subject to other contemporaneous changes, so I investigate the mechanism in the cross section, leveraging bank-level heterogeneity in how much lower interest rates have compressed deposit spreads. Banks whose deposit rates have been historically stickier suffered larger losses on their deposit provision business. Consistent with the extension of the model with heterogeneous banks, in the recent low-rate environment these banks also experienced lower growth in retained earnings, equity (meaning that they did not adjust payouts to offset the lost deposit spread income), and lending, and a larger increase in loan spreads.
A burgeoning literature studies the short-run transmission of negative interest rates set by monetary policy. In contemporaneous and independent work, Abadi, Brunnermeier, and Koby (2023) study monetary policy shocks in a model featuring bank market power in loan and deposit markets, maturity mismatch, and financial frictions. They show the existence of a “reversal rate” in lending, defined as the rate below which further policy rate cuts have a negative marginal effect on lending. My paper shares some economic mechanisms, such as the contrasting roles of asset revaluation and net interest income, but features important differences relative to Abadi, Brunnermeier, and Koby (2023) and other papers studying the transmission of negative monetary policy rates.2
First, my model centers around the competition between money and deposits causing deposit spreads to shrink when rates fall, even well above the ZLB, in line with Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017). Since deposit spreads are a major source of bank income, this ultimately affects bank net worth and lending. By contrast, in Abadi, Brunnermeier, and Koby (2023), Ulate (2021), and Eggertsson et al. (2023), low rates do not compress deposit spreads until interest rates turn negative, as money (i.e., cash) is irrelevant at positive rates and its only role is to impose a ZLB on deposit rates. This distinction is captured by the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$in my model below which lower rates start to have harmful effects on bank lending. Note thati¯$\bar{i}$is positive by construction and potentially quite high, as it is the rate below which deposit spreads become too low to sustain banks' payout policies.3
Second, this focus on positive rates allows me to study the consequences of the secular decline in the natural interest rater∗$r^{*}$, that is, a slow-moving trend driven by fundamental shifts in preferences and technology, whereas the literature warns against excessive monetary policy accommodation in New Keynesian models, holdingr∗$r^{*}$fixed. I motivate this different approach with aggregate and bank-level evidence on the long-run behavior of deposit and loan spreads.4The focus on long-run structural issues also suggests novel normative analyses, such as in SectionII.Bstudying the trade-off between reducing the cost of liquidity (as in the standard Friedman rule logic) and stimulating lending when setting a long-run inflation target.
My paper also relates to recent work on banks' exposure to monetary policy in the presence of both maturity mismatch and liquidity premia on deposits (Di Tella and Kurlat (2021), Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2021)). I propose and document a complementary explanation for the stability of bank net interest margins (NIMs) based on the offsetting behavior of loan and deposit spreads. Accounting for the two kinds of spreads helps explain why NIMs have been so stable historically. Most importantly, studying the two spreads together is key to understanding how the secular decline in U.S. interest rates impacts not only banks, but also the nonfinancial sector, as loan spreads affect the cost of credit faced by banks' borrowers.5My long-run results are consistent with a recent body of work that tries to measure the importance of banks' lending and deposit-taking businesses, in terms of profits, returns, or stock market valuations. Egan, Lewellen, and Sunderam (2021) apply structural methods from production function estimation to the banking sector and find meaningful synergies between deposit taking and lending in the cross section of U.S. banks. Schwert (2020) finds that banks earn a large premium over the market price of credit risk.
The rate environment following the Great Financial Crisis (GFC) has spurred an explosion of work on the effects of low interest rates on banks, especially in Europe. Claessens, Coleman, and Donnelly (2018) study the relationship between interest rates and NIMs. Altavilla, Boucinha, and Peydró (2018) examine how standard and nonstandard monetary policy measures affect European banks' profitability. Ampudia and Van den Heuvel (2022) find a state-dependent impact of monetary shocks on bank stock prices in Europe. The empirical evidence on negative rates and bank lending (e.g., Heider, Saidi, and Schepens (2019), Altavilla et al. (2022) Bottero et al. (2022), Eggertsson et al. (2023)) is mixed, as surveyed in Heider, Saidi, and Schepens (2021). My paper suggests one way to reconcile the diverse results: rate cuts stimulate lending in the short run (albeit less so at lower rates), but hurt lending in the long run. Indeed, Arce et al. (2020) recently find that Spanish banks reduce lending and increase spreads only after a few years of negative rates. Prolonged low interest rates in advanced economies have spurred concerns about declining bank profitability, weaker bank balance sheets (Committee on the Global Financial System (2018)), and impaired monetary policy transmission. Wang et al. (2022) develop and estimate a structural model to quantify the effect of loan and deposit market power on monetary policy transmission, in particular at low interest rates. de Groot and Haas (2023) show that when the central bank has a preference for smoothing, negative rates can be useful as a commitment device to forward guidance in spite of the current harmful effect on banks. Onofri, Peersman, and Smets (2023) show that whether negative interest rates are contractionary or expansionary depends on the assets available to households, if deposits are subject to a lower bound but other assets are not.
Seminal papers on the “bank lending channel” of monetary policy (Bernanke and Blinder (1988,1992), Kashyap and Stein (1995)) have relied on reserve requirements. Bianchi and Bigio (2022) show how loan spreads depend on central bank policies, such as the rate paid on reserves, that affect the cost of bank liquidity management since reserves must be used for settlement. I share Van den Heuvel (2002) and Gertler and Karadi's (2011) emphasis on scarce bank equity instead. Piazzesi, Rogers, and Schneider (2021) discuss the interplay between inside money (deposits) that facilitates end-user transactions, as in this paper, and outside money (reserves) used in interbank transactions. They abstract from lending and from the substitution or competition between inside and outside money that is key to my paper. Zentefis (2020) shows that the pass-through of monetary policy to loan rates can break down when banks have too little capital to compete with each other in a Salop model.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdevelops the baseline model, highlighting banks' dual role as credit and liquidity providers. SectionIIexamines the model's implications for the relationship between low interest rates, intermediation spreads, and bank lending. SectionIIIpresents aggregate and cross-sectional evidence supporting the model's predictions. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Model: Banks as Credit and Liquidity:
I present a model of banks with a dual role: they lend to borrowers without access to capital markets, and they issue deposits providing liquidity services to savers. The two main sources of bank income—credit spreads on loans and liquidity premia on deposits—are entangled through banks' financial constraints.
Time is discretet=0,1,⋯$t=0,1,\dots$Banks are firms that intermediate funds between two types of households (borrowers and savers). On the asset side, banks can hold bonds or finance loans of arbitrary maturity to borrowers, while on the liability side they can issue bonds or short-term deposits. Households can save in bonds, deposits, or cash. Cash and deposits provide liquidity services relative to bonds. Monetary policy sets the nominal interest rate on bonds, while the rates on loans and deposits adjust endogenously. Spreads between loan rates and bond rates, and between bond rates and deposit rates, can persist because banks are subject to financial constraints.
The final good is produced linearly from laborN$N$with linear technologyYt=AtNt$Y_{t}=A_{t}N_{t}$. Prices are flexible and firms are competitive, and therefore the real wage isAt$A_{t}$and firms make zero profits.
At each datet$t$, the traded assets are short-term bonds with date-t+1$t+1$face valueat+1$a_{t+1}$, moneymt+1$m_{t+1}$, short-term bank depositsdt+1$d_{t+1}$, and bank loanslt,t+k$l_{t,t+k}$with maturityk∈1,2,⋯,K$k\in \left\lbrace 1,2,\dots,K\right\rbrace $. There is no risk, but assets can pay different rates for two reasons. First, different assets are associated with different borrowing constraints. For instance, bank-dependent borrowers are able to issue loans but not bonds, and households cannot save in loans directly—only banks have the expertise to manage loans. Second, money and deposits are valued not only for their pecuniary returns, but also for the transaction services they provide.
Their respective date-t$t$net real yields are denoted byrt$r_{t}$,rtm=1(1+πt+1)−1$r_{t}^{m}=\frac{1}{(1+\pi _{t+1})}-1$, whereπt+1$\pi _{t+1}$is the inflation rate betweent$t$andt+1$t+1$,rtd$r_{t}^{d}$, andrt,t+kl$r_{t,t+k}^{l}$.6For the special case of short-term loans (k=1$k=1$), I simply writertl=rt,t+1l$r_{t}^{l}=r_{t,t+1}^{l}$.
The nominal interest rate on bonds isit=(1+rt)(1+πt+1)−1$i_{t}=(1+r_{t})(1+\pi _{t+1})-1$, which is assumed to be positive throughout the paper. The nominal rates on deposits and short-term loans are denoted, respectively, byitd$i_{t}^{d}$anditl$i_{t}^{l}$, and satisfy1+itd=(1+πt+1)(1+rtd)$1+i_{t}^{d}=(1+\pi _{t+1})(1+r_{t}^{d})$and1+itl=(1+πt+1)(1+rtl)$1+i_{t}^{l}=(1+\pi _{t+1})(1+r_{t}^{l})$.
Households come in two types. “Savers” are unconstrained households that can be viewed as also incorporating all of the borrowers in the economy who do not depend on banks. “Borrowers” comprise the remaining bank-dependent borrowers.
Assumption 1. (Liquidity)The liquidity aggregatorxm,d$x\left(m,d\right)$is strictly increasing in both arguments, homothetic, differentiable, and concave.
The limitUx→0$U_{x}\rightarrow 0$corresponds to a model without transaction frictions and thus zero liquidity premia,it=std=0$i_{t}=s_{t}^{d}=0$. I refer to this case as the model without liquidity frictions.
To generate loan demands while limiting the number of state variables, I assume that borrowing from banks is driven entirely by household lifecycle motives.7
Banks' maturity mismatch will lead to differences between short-run and long-run effects of interest rate changes. To capture different loan maturities, I use a “preferred habitat” framework: there are overlapping generations of borrowers who are heterogeneous with respect to the maturity of the loans they need. At each datet$t$, a massμk$\mu _{k}$of type-k$k$borrowers, indexed by their life spank∈1,⋯,K$k\in \left\lbrace 1,\dots,K\right\rbrace $, is born. Each type-k$k$borrower lives only at two dates,t$t$andt+k$t+k$, and is endowed withn¯k$\overline{n}^{k}$units of labor when old, so needs to borrow to consume when young. If borrowers could freely issue bonds at priceqt$q_{t}$bank loans would be irrelevant. The following financial friction gives a role to banks' asset side.
Assumption 2. (Credit frictions)Borrowers cannot issue bonds and must borrow through bank loans.8

The total endowment of labor is constant and equal to one:∑k=1Kμkn¯k+μsn¯s=1$\sum _{k=1}^{K}\mu _{k}\overline{n}^{k}+\mu ^{s}\overline{n}^{s}=1$.
I closely follow the workhorse model of banks developed by Gertler and Kiyotaki (2010). The main difference is that here not only are banks specialists in credit provision, but they also issue liabilities valued for their liquidity.
Assumption 3. (Bank net payouts)In each period, a random massρ∼$\tilde{\rho }$of banks exits and a mass of banksρ∼$\tilde{\rho }$enters, each of them with exogenous startup equityζtEt/ρ∼$\zeta _{t}E_{t}/\tilde{\rho }$. Each exiting bank sells its loan portfolio to the remaining banks and then rebates its equity to the representative saver. Net payouts are high enough for banking to be relevant in steady state:

Equation (3) states that banks are effectively “more impatient” than households, which is a standard assumption in the macrofinance literature (e.g., Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014)). The wedge betweenβ$\beta$and1−ρ$1-\rho$is crucial in this model designed to study long-run spreads—without it, financial frictions would vanish over time as banks build up equity from their profits.
In the baseline model, aggregate net bank payouts are exogenous, equal toDivt=ρEt$Div_{t}=\rho E_{t}$from Assumption3. InInternet AppendixSectionIII.C, I endogenizeζ$\zeta$and thusρ$\rho$with a model of equity issuance frictions, and I derive how costly issuance must be for condition (3) to hold.
Assumption 4. (Limited pledgeability)At datet$t$, banks can pledge only a fractionθ<1$\theta <1$of date-t+1$t+1$assets to cover their liabilities.11
The limited pledgeability friction implies a transparent form for the leverage constraint.
Lemma 1.In equilibrium, banks only issue deposit liabilities,liabt=dt+11+rtd$\text{liab}_{t}=\frac{d_{t+1}}{1+r_{t}^{d}}$, and the leverage constraint is always binding withϕ¯t=θ(1+rtl)/(1+rtd)1−θ(1+rtl)/(1+rtd)$\overline{\phi }_{t}=\frac{\theta (1+r_{t}^{l})/(1+r_{t}^{d})}{1-\theta (1+r_{t}^{l})/(1+r_{t}^{d})}$.

Thus, a higher spread between loan ratesrtl$r_{t}^{l}$and deposit ratesrtd$r_{t}^{d}$relaxes the bank leverage constraint att$t$by making more net interest income pledgeable att+1$t+1$. This captures a positive dependence of bank lending capacity in current profits. The results hold more generally than with this specific leverage constraint: what is needed is simply thatϕ¯t$\overline{\phi }_{t}$is nondecreasing in bank profits (which includes the case of a constantϕ¯$\overline{\phi }$).
I use a standard equilibrium concept that assumes flexible prices and full employment to study long-run issues.
LetLt=Lt+ktk=0K−1$\mathbf {L}^{t}=\left\lbrace L_{t+k}^{t}\right\rbrace _{k=0}^{K-1}$be the aggregate stock of loans outstanding at the beginning of periodt$t$, whereLt+st$L_{t+s}^{t}$is the sum of individual loan positionslt+k$l_{t+k}$over banks that are active att−1$t-1$. The economy hasK+2$K+2$aggregate state variables, summarized by the vectorZt=ats,dts,Lt$\mathbf {Z}_{t}=\left[a_{t}^{s},d_{t}^{s},\mathbf {L}^{t}\right]$, whereats$a_{t}^{s}$anddts$d_{t}^{s}$denote savers' bond and deposit holdings, respectively.
Definition 1. (Flexible-prices equilibrium)Given initial conditionsZ0$\mathbf {Z}_{0}$, a path for monetary policyitt≥0$\left\lbrace i_{t}\right\rbrace _{t\ge 0}$, and an initial price levelP0>0$P_{0}>0$, a flexible-prices equilibrium is a sequence of allocationsctii,Zt,mtt≥0$\left\lbrace \left\lbrace c_{t}^{i}\right\rbrace _{i},\mathbf {Z}_{t},m_{t}\right\rbrace _{t\ge 0}$and ratesrt,rtd,rt,t+klk=1Kt≥0$\left\lbrace r_{t},r_{t}^{d},\left\lbrace r_{t,t+k}^{l}\right\rbrace _{k=1}^{K}\right\rbrace _{t\ge 0}$such that firms, households, and banks optimize, and markets for goods and all assets clear.

I now discuss the key markets in this model, namely, the market for liquidity (money and deposits) and the market for loans.
In addition to their intertemporal consumption-savings problem, savers face a static optimal liquidity demand problem that has the following solution.
Lemma 2. (Liquidity demand)Denotebt≡std/it$b_{t}\equiv s_{t}^{d}/i_{t}$. The optimal money-deposit ratio is


I assume that the two forms of liquidity are sufficiently close substitutes.
Assumption 5. (Gross substitutes)Money and deposits are gross substitutes, in the sense thatxd(f,1)x(f,1)$\frac{x_{d}(f,1)}{x(f,1)}$is nonincreasing inf$f$.

Deposits and cash are two competing sources of liquidity. As can be seen in (1), each has a “price” equal to the opportunity cost relative to bonds:std$s_{t}^{d}$for deposits andit$i_{t}$for cash. Under Assumption5(which is equivalent toε≥1$\epsilon \ge 1$in the CES case), deposit demandD$\mathcal {D}$is not only decreasing in the price of its own liquidity servicesstd$s_{t}^{d}$but also increasing init$i_{t}$, the price of the competing liquidity. In SectionII, I show the implications of this competition once we endogenize the supply of private liquidity (through the dynamics of bank capital) and combine it with equilibrium in loan markets.
At any datet$t$, the economy can be in one of two regimes. Importantly, in both regimes banks' leverage constraint (4) binds, because banks always want to issue as many deposits as possible to earn the deposit spread. What distinguishes the two regimes is the equilibrium in theloanmarket.
In the unconstrained lending regime, the demand for new loans is lower than banks' lending capacity at datet$t$. Bonds and loans must then be perfect substitutes from the banks' viewpoint, and there is no credit spread, that is,rtl=rt$r_{t}^{l}=r_{t}$. In the constrained lending regime, date-t$t$credit demand would exceed banks' lending capacity absent a spread, and thus a positive spread has to open up to clear the credit market, that is,rtl>rt$r_{t}^{l}>r_{t}$. All else equal, lower equity will shrink the size of banks' balance sheets and push the economy into the constrained lending regime, which is key to give a macroeconomic role to banks.13
Directly putting liquidity services in the utility function is the simplest way to generate a demand for assets with dominated return; another approach would be to explicitly model transaction frictions, but many such models (e.g., cash-in-advance constraints) are isomorphic to assuming liquidity in the utility (Feenstra (1986)). I follow Chetty (1969), Poterba and Rotemberg (1987), and more recently Nagel (2016), Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), and Di Tella and Kurlat (2021), who all incorporate two substitutable forms of liquidity through an aggregatorx$x$. In the background,x$x$might stand for inattentive depositors or search costs (see Driscoll and Judson (2013), Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), Yankov (2024)).
Nonsatiation of liquidity services from deposits implies a positive deposit spreadstd$s_{t}^{d}$for any finite level of deposits.14As long as there is a positive deposit spread, it would be profitable for unconstrained, competitive banks to issue more deposits and invest the proceeds in bonds or loans. Without any further assumption, there would be no equilibrium when liquidity from utility cannot be satiated, since banks would then want to issue an infinite amount of deposits. There are essentially two ways to rationalize the coexistence of a positive deposit spread and a finite amount of deposits that we observe in the data. One is to assume, as in most of the macrofinance literature, that banks face a leverage constraint that prevents them from increasing the size of their balance sheet as much as they would like. In my baseline model, I focus on this leverage constraint friction.
The other route is to assume, as in Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), that the deposit market is imperfectly competitive, and banks willingly restrict the supply of deposits to earn higher profits. In Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), the optimal deposit spreadsd$s^{d}$can be positive (and the optimal deposit supply finite) only if the number of banksN$N$is low enough and deposits and consumption are sufficiently complementary. The precise condition for banks to setsd>0$s^{d}>0$isN<1−ρη−1+1$N<\frac{1-\rho }{\eta -1}+1$whereρ$\rho$is the elasticity of substitution between consumption and liquidity andη$\eta$is the elasticity of substitution between varieties of bank deposits. Solving this model requires abstracting from the strong income effects resulting from complementarity by taking the limit of zero liquidityχ→0$\chi \rightarrow 0$. Here I use a standard separable specification (corresponding toρ=1$\rho =1$) to make general equilibrium with positive liquidity tractable. This implies that even a monopolist bank (N=1$N=1$) would like to setsd=0$s^{d}=0$and thereforeD→∞$D\rightarrow \infty$, unless banks eventually face another constraint such as the leverage constraint. A valuable extension outside the scope of the paper would be to combine complementarity and deposit market power with a leverage constraint.
The baseline model assumes that financial flows occur only between households. Another possibility would be to have constrained firms borrow from households in order to invest.15InInternet AppendixSectionIII.E, I consider a variant of the model in which bank loans finance firm investment, and thus capital and GDP are affected by financial frictions.
I assume that banks have a two-sided business. The literature (Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002)) provides microeconomic arguments for synergies between loans (or more precisely credit lines) and deposits.Internet AppendixSectionIII.Bhighlights a different “macroeconomic” synergy: when these two activities are unbundled in the sense that different intermediaries provide credit and liquidity, deposit and loan spreads become disconnected from each other and unaffected by the level of nominal interest rates, but both spreads are higher.
article.II. Low Interest Rates and Long-Run Bank:
In this section, I present the main results. A permanent decline in the nominal interest ratei$i$compresses deposit spreads. Oncei$i$falls below a thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$, the lower deposit spreads lead to a decrease in bank loan supply and an increase in loan spreads. Moreover, the short-run and long-run responses of lending can differ substantially, with lending increasing on impact but eventually converging to a lower level.
I suppose that productivity (and hence output) grows at a constant gross rateG$G$, and consider steady-state equilibria. A steady state is an equilibrium in which real quantities divided by outputYt$Y_{t}$and asset prices are constant. Quantities without time subscripts are normalized byYt$Y_{t}$, that is,x≡xtYt$x\equiv \frac{x_{t}}{Y_{t}}$. I only consider steady states with positive bank equity and ignore the unstable “financial autarky” steady state withE=0$E=0$.
The main experiment is a permanent decrease in the nominal ratei$i$, that is, a “secular decline in interest rates.” This can happen in response to a lower real interest rater∗$r^{*}$and/or lower inflationπ$\pi$. The real rater∗$r^{*}$, in turn, can fall due to lower growthG$G$and/or a higher discount factorβ$\beta$. In formal results below I focus on shocks to inflationπ$\pi$or growthG$G$as they have unambiguous effects. Increases inβ$\beta$are more complex to study analytically but I find similar effects numerically.
Proposition 1.Fixβ$\beta$and consider changes in the nominal ratei$i$driven byG$G$orπ$\pi$.
Neutral case: Loan and deposit spreads and lending are independent ofi$i$ifxd(f,1)x(f,)1$\frac{x_{d}(f,1)}{x(f,)1}$is constant inf$f$, which is the case when the aggregatorx$x$is Cobb-Douglas, that is,x(m,d)=mηd1−η$x(m,d)=m^{\eta }d^{1-\eta }$for someη∈[0,1]$\eta \in [0,1]$.
Money and deposit strict gross substitutes: Suppose thatxd(f,1)x(f,1)$\frac{x_{d}(f,1)}{x(f,1)}$is strictly decreasing inf$f$. Then there exists a thresholdi¯>0$\bar{i}>0$such that the economy is in the constrained lending regime if and only ifi<i¯$i<\bar{i}$. Moreover, a decline ini$i$has the following effects:


Proposition1(proved inInternet AppendixSectionIV, together with all the other results) reflects the competition in liquidity provision between two types of providers: the government, which provides public liquiditym$m$at pricei$i$, and banks, which provide private liquidityd$d$at pricesd$s^{d}$. A lower nominal interest rate corresponds to a lower price of public liquidity, which intensifies the competition faced by banks. In equilibrium, the deposit spreadsd$s^{d}$must decrease along with the nominal interest ratei$i$.
The transmission to lending works through banks' financial constraints. Deposit spreads act as a form of “private seigniorage” accruing to banks. The lower deposit spreads resulting from a decline ini$i$tighten banks' financial constraints, by reducing retained earnings and ultimately net worth. This, in turn, harms bank-dependent borrowers, who can borrow less and face a higher loan spread.
The threshold interest ratei¯$\bar{i}$is a central concept in this paper. It is the nominal interest rate below which banks cannot earn sufficient income from deposit spreads alone to meet their required return on equity and support the unconstrained level of lending. This means thati¯$\bar{i}$is necessarily positive and potentially quite high, which is in stark contrast with the literature on negative policy rates (e.g., Ulate (2021), Abadi, Brunnermeier, and Koby (2023), Eggertsson et al. (2023)) that shows harmful effects of lower rates on bank lending when deposit rates run into the ZLB. The key difference is that in these other models, money is irrelevant at positive rates: it is not held and does not affect how banks set deposit rates. Its only role is to prevent deposit rates from falling below zero. Here, the competition between money and deposits affects deposit spreads and thus bank income even at positive rates.
The next result characterizesi¯$\bar{i}$and shows comparative statics with respect to the parameters capturing the supply side (i.e., the static and dynamic financial frictions affecting how much liquidity and credit banks can produce) as well as the demand for liquidity and in particular the role of money.
Proposition 2.Suppose thatm$m$andd$d$are strict gross substitutes and the utility from liquidity is sufficiently high, that is,

Comparative statics:i¯$\bar{i}$increases with banks' required excess returnν$\nu$, decreases with pledgeabilityθ$\theta$, and decreases with liquidity benefitsχ$\chi$.
Frictionless limits:i¯$\bar{i}$converges to zero (i.e., lending becomes unconstrained at any interest rate) ifν→0$\nu \rightarrow 0$(costless equity issuance),θ→1$\theta \rightarrow 1$(unlimited bank leverage), orxm(m,d)→0$x_{m}(m,d)\rightarrow 0$for allm,d$m,d$(money becomes irrelevant, e.g.,α→0$\alpha \rightarrow 0$when x is CES).
CES special case: Ifx$x$is CES (9), thenΦ(y)=α/(1−α)1/y−11ε−1$\Phi (y)=\left[\frac{\alpha /(1-\alpha)}{1/y-1}\right]^{\frac{1}{\epsilon -1}}$andi¯$\bar{i}$decreases withε$\epsilon$ifχ≤χmin1−α$\chi \le \frac{\chi _{\min }}{1-\alpha }$.

A decrease inχ$\chi$can be interpreted as an improvement in payment technology that reduces households' need to hold low-yield liquid assets such as money and deposits to facilitate transactions. This hurts banks' deposit business and leads to an increase ini¯$\bar{i}$. If liquidity benefits disappear (χ→0$\chi \rightarrow 0$), then deposit spreads must vanish completely and bank income can only come from loan spreads, and thus no unconstrained lending regime is possible andi¯=∞$\bar{i}=\infty$. More generally, the conditionχ≥χmin$\chi \ge \chi _{\min }$requires that savers value liquidity sufficiently for deposit spreads to potentially sustain the unconstrained level of lending. In the baseline calibration,χ>χmin$\chi >\chi _{\min }$and thusi¯$\bar{i}$will be finite and equal to approximately 8%.
Figure2shows how the different parameters affecti¯$\bar{i}$quantitatively. The comparative statics are intuitive: ifθ→1$\theta \rightarrow 1$orν→0$\nu \rightarrow 0$, then financial frictions disappear andi¯→0$\bar{i}\rightarrow 0$, that is, lending becomes unconstrained at any interest rate.
In the CES case, a lower elasticity of substitutionε$\epsilon$leads to a higher thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$ifχ≤χmin1−α$\chi \le \frac{\chi _{\min }}{1-\alpha }$, which is the empirically plausible case sinceα$\alpha$will be very close to one. A lower elasticityε$\epsilon$means that deposit demand is less sensitive toi$i$(the price of the competing liquidity), and hence a larger nominal ratei¯$\bar{i}$is required for deposits to rise sufficiently to support the unconstrained level of lending. Thus, negative effects on lending appear at a higher rate than under perfect substitutability between money and deposits (ε=∞$\epsilon =\infty$).
Remark 1.Proposition2shows that the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$is positive only if some frictions to equity issuance persist forever, that is, if the excess returnν$\nu$remains positive in steady state. In this sense, the lending effects described in this paper must be viewed as an upper bound on the actual long-run effects, with lower effects if equity issuance frictions weaken over time andν$\nu$eventually falls.Internet AppendixSectionIII.Cextends the model to allow for a costly adjustment of net payouts and thus an endogenousν$\nu$, but still assuming that the cost of equity issuance does not disappear in the long run.

An additional result is that in the low rates region (0<i<i¯$0<i<\bar{i}$), the long-run pass-through of interest rates to deposit and loan rates increases with rates. This state-dependence bridges the gap between the full pass-through at high interest rates (i>i¯$i>\bar{i}$) and the extreme case of zero pass-through when rates turn negative (i<0$i<0$) considered in the literature.
Proposition 3. (Deposit and loan rate convexity)Suppose thati<i¯$i<\bar{i}$and the nominal ratei$i$changes due to lower inflationπ$\pi$or lower growthG$G$. To first-order ini$i$, the nominal deposit rateid$i^{d}$follows


An increase ini$i$acts as a positive demand shock to deposit demandD(sd,i)$\mathcal {D}(s^{d},i)$. Proposition3builds on the fact that in the long-run deposit market equilibrium, the price of deposit liquiditysd$s^{d}$increases less than one-for-one in response to the higher price of moneyi$i$, and hence their ratiosd/i$s^{d}/i$falls. Otherwise, ifsd/i$s^{d}/i$remained constant, deposit demand would end up contracting sinceD(sd,i)$\mathcal {D}(s^{d},i)$is homogeneous of degree −1, and hence equal to1iDsdi,1$\frac{1}{i}\mathcal {D}\left(\frac{s^{d}}{i},1\right)$. Thus, the intuition is that the general equilibrium response ofsd$s^{d}$does not overturn the initial positive demand shock induced byi$i$.
This intuition suggests that the result applies to more general deposit demand functionsD(sd,i)$\mathcal {D}(s^{d},i)$than (10), and more general long-run deposit supply functions than the one implied by my model's specific banking dynamics. Indeed, in the proof I show thatsd/i$s^{d}/i$decreases withi$i$as long as (i) an increase in bothsd$s^{d}$andi$i$in the same proportions would contract deposit demand, that is, ifD(λsd,λi)$\mathcal {D}(\lambda s^{d},\lambda i)$is a decreasing function ofλ$\lambda$(which can be interpreted as a standard income effect sinceD$\mathcal {D}$is an uncompensated demand), and (ii) long-run deposit supply is nondecreasing insd$s^{d}$, as is standard for supply curves. For instance, in the simplest case of fixed long-run deposit supply, deposit demandD$\mathcal {D}$would fall ifsd$s^{d}$andi$i$increased in proportion, and thus the market can clear only if the deposit spreadsd$s^{d}$rises by less thani$i$, which implies thatβd(i)$\beta ^{d}(i)$increases withi$i$.
Moreover, the tight link between loan spreadssl$s^{l}$and deposit spreadssd$s^{d}$implies that the loan rate inherits the convexity of the deposit rate, with a loan rate betaβl$\beta ^{l}$that is also increasing ini$i$. Loan rates have received less empirical attention than deposit rates, but one example of loan rate convexity is the prevalence of “interest rate floors” in leveraged loans at low rates (i.e., clauses ensuring a minimum interest rate on otherwise floating rate loans), as documented by Arscott (2018) and Roberts and Schwert (2022). In equation (16), the intercept term ati→0$i\rightarrow 0$,log1+ν1+θν$\log \left(\frac{1+\nu }{1+\theta \nu }\right)$, can be interpreted as such a floor.
To illustrate the model's results numerically, I use a baseline calibration summarized in TableIand detailed inInternet AppendixSectionII.
Figure1shows the steady-state net nominal rates for loans and deposits as a function of the nominal bond ratei$i$. The left panel holdsπ$\pi$fixed and variesG$G$, while the right panel holdsG$G$fixed and variesπ$\pi$. The equilibrium loan and deposit rates are the same whether the variation ini$i$is due toG$G$orπ$\pi$. Oncei$i$falls below the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$, further decreases in nominal rates lead to lower deposit spreads and higher loan spreads. In this calibration, the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$below which lending is constrained is at 8%. The figure also highlights the convexity of deposit and loan rates as a function ofi$i$discussed in Proposition3.
Figure2illustrates the comparative statics of the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$described in Proposition2, which does indeed converge to zero asρ→1−β$\rho \rightarrow 1-\beta$(or equivalentlyν→0$\nu \rightarrow 0$),θ→1$\theta \rightarrow 1$, orα→0$\alpha \rightarrow 0$. The threshold under the baseline calibrationi¯≈8%$\bar{i}\approx 8\%$may seem high relative to the literature that studies harmful lending effects appearing at negative interest rates, but as explained above this is due in part to my model focusing on the competition between money and deposits that already takes place at positive rates. In particular, the seemingly high value ofi¯$\bar{i}$results from targeting realistic money holdings, which requires a weight on moneyα$\alpha$very close to one to rationalize the quantity of money held in spite of observed deposit rates, whereas the literature on negative rates (e.g., Ulate (2021), Abadi, Brunnermeier, and Koby (2023), Eggertsson et al. (2023)) abstracts from money holdings at positive interest rates. Figure2shows thati¯$\bar{i}$could be significantly lower under a lowerα$\alpha$than the calibrated valueα=0.99$\alpha =0.99$, given the extremely high slope ofi¯$\bar{i}$at highα$\alpha$. However, this would imply much lower money holdings than observed in the data. A lower bank payout rateρ$\rho$or a higher pledgeability parameterθ$\theta$would also decreasei¯$\bar{i}$, but changing these parameters would also increase the size of the banking sector (in terms of deposits relative to GDP) or increase bank leverage relative to the baseline calibration.
Figure3shows the steady-state quantities (normalized by GDP) for deposits, loans, bonds, equity, leverageϕ$\phi$, and cash holdingsm$m$. The equilibrium quantities are also the same whether the variation ini$i$is due toG$G$orπ$\pi$. Oncei$i$falls below the thresholdi¯$\bar{i}$, further decreases in nominal rates lead to a contraction in lending, deposits, and equity, and a rise in cash holdings. Since leverage is equal toϕ¯=θ(1+sl)(1+sd)1−θ(1+sl)(1+sd)$\overline{\phi }=\frac{\theta (1+s^{l})(1+s^{d})}{1-\theta (1+s^{l})(1+s^{d})}$, the decrease inϕ¯$\overline{\phi }$means that the higher loan spreadsl$s^{l}$does not fully offset the smaller deposit spreadsd$s^{d}$, although the decrease inϕ¯$\overline{\phi }$is very mild, which shows that the sumsl+sd$s^{l}+s^{d}$is roughly constant. FiguresIA.2andIA.3show similar patterns for changes inβ$\beta$.19
How do the model's predictions compare to the data? In SectionIII.A, I show that the behavior ofprices, that is, loan and deposit spreads, in response to lower rates is consistent with the model; I also provide evidence on cross-sectional responses of quantities (i.e., lending) based on bank-level heterogeneity in deposit pass-through. The behavior ofaggregate quantitiesis more difficult to rationalize. While there is indeed a negative relationship between nominal rates and cash holdings (as in the traditional long-run money demand literature; see, e.g., Lucas and Nicolini (2015)), there are two main differences between the model and the data: banks have grown in size in parallel with the secular decline in interest rates, and banks' deposits and bondholdings are high at low interest rates.
Mapping the model to the data is challenging in a nonstationary world since the model predicts a contraction in the steady-state quantity of bank deposits and credit. But as emphasized by Greenwood and Scharfstein (2013) or Philippon (2015), the U.S. financial sector is far from being in steady state: bank assets over GDP grew steadily from 53% in 1980 to 83% in 2018, reflecting growing demand for intermediation. The model assumes that the economy has reached a balanced growth path with stable intermediation volumes over GDP, but its predictions regarding these quantities should be interpreted relative to the upward trend due to shifting demand, which can be strong enough to mask the effect of low rates on lending volumes.20Moreover, SectionII.Cshows that short-run and long-run effects of lower rates work in opposite directions. Recent trends may therefore still be consistent with the model.
In addition, bank balance sheets are highly simplified in the model. In practice, bonds (securities and reserves) have lower risk weights than loans in standard capital requirements, whereas the binding leverage constraint (4) in the model resembles the supplemental leverage ratio (SLR) in the United States which puts equal weights on all asset classes (see Bolton et al. (2020) for an analysis of the potential downsides of deposit inflows due to the SLR). An extension for future research would be to allow for lower risk weights on bonds. This would lead to additional “regimes” (beyond the unconstrained and constrained lending regimes) in which banks would be willing to hold bonds in spite of high loan spreads, due to the higher pledgeability of bonds allowing them to expand their deposit base to earn more in deposit spreads. In Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), bonds have effectively no risk weight and thus the limiting factor on bank size and deposits is not equity but rather deposit market power, which leads banks to increase deposits at low rates.21
The strong link between deposit and loan spreads has normative implications for the optimal long-run inflation target, calling for a departure from the Friedman rule that maximizes the utility from liquidity. Without credit frictions, that is, if borrowers can freely issue bonds, all agents' consumption levels are independent of policy and the central bank simply maximizes the utility from liquidity services,χlogx$\chi \log x$. This is achieved by following the Friedman rule, that is, by making the price of liquidity services as small as possible by settingi→0$i\rightarrow 0$.22
By contrast, the model with credit frictions implies a trade-off between deposit and loan spreads: setting a low inflation target and thus a low nominal rate has the standard benefit of reducing the cost of liquidity, but has a welfare cost for borrowers who must face higher loan spreads.
Figure4shows welfare for both groups as the steady-state inflation targetπ$\pi$varies, holdingr∗$r^{*}$fixed. Since there are redistributional concerns, the optimal policy maximizingλsWs+(1−λs)Wb$\lambda ^{s}W^{s}+(1-\lambda ^{s})W^{b}$depends on the welfare weightλs$\lambda ^{s}$on savers. For high welfare weights on savers, the optimal policy remains the Friedman rule, setting inflation such thati→0$i\rightarrow 0$(i.e.,π→−r∗$\pi \rightarrow -r^{*}$) to satiate liquidity demand. For high welfare weights on borrowers, the optimal policy is to satiate loan demandL(sl)$\mathcal {L}(s^{l})$instead, by settingi→i¯$i\rightarrow \bar{i}$, which happens aroundπ→4%$\pi \rightarrow 4\%$in Figure4.
Proposition1focuses on long-run effects. Turning to the transitional dynamics from one steady state to another with lower interest rates, Figure5illustrates a central distinction highlighted in this paper: interest rate cuts have different effects on bank lending and deposits in the short run and the long run.
A permanent decrease in the real rater∗$r^{*}$can stimulate bank lending in the short run because capital gains on banks' outstanding long-term assets boost bank equity. This relaxes the leverage constraint and allows banks to temporarily increase borrowing and lending. Over time, however, the initial increase in lending vanishes as long-term assets mature, while the persistent decline in deposit spreads does not stop and drives bank lending to a lower steady-state level that features higher loan spreads, as discussed above. But the model predicts that contractionary effects take time to dominate the initial expansionary effects.24
While in the long run Proposition1shows that changes inG$G$andπ$\pi$have identical effects, transitional dynamics differ. The initial positive effect on lending occurs only for real rate (r∗$r^{*}$) shocks. By contrast, FigureIA.4shows that a permanent decrease inπ$\pi$contracts deposits and lending immediately, because real bond rates are approximately unchanged and therefore the revaluation effects are absent.
The strong difference between short-run and long-run effects is driven by the combination of maturity mismatch and the form of the bank leverage constraint (4).
To isolate the role of maturity mismatch, we can consider an economy with the same leverage constraint but only short-term loans, for which it is possible to solve analytically the path of the transition to the new steady state. The following analytical result shows that without maturity mismatch, short-run and long-run effects are both negative.
Proposition 4. (No maturity mismatch)Suppose that all loans are short-term,K=1$K=1$. Consider a permanent decline in the nominal interest ratei$i$due to either lower growthG<G−$G<G_{-}$or lower inflationπ<π−$\pi <\pi _{-}$. Then, initial lending always falls relative to the previous steady state:l11+r0l<l−1+r−l$\frac{l_{1}}{1+r_{0}^{l}}<\frac{l_{-}}{1+r_{-}^{l}}$. If the decline ini$i$is due to a higher discount factorβ>β−$\beta >\beta _{-}$, then initial lending falls if the elasticity of substitutionε$\epsilon$between money and deposits is sufficiently high or if the initial nominal rate is sufficient low.25

We can show numerically that the intuition extends to intermediate cases of maturity mismatch. Figure6varies banks' balance sheets by reducing the fraction of long-term loans. Each line corresponds to a different average asset duration, going from four years (slightly below the baseline 4.6 years) to one year, which corresponds to only short-term loans as in Proposition4. The short-run positive lending response weakens as maturity mismatch decreases, eventually disappearing completely.26
Accounting rules record the difference betweenet$e_{t}$andbt$b_{t}$as accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI). As part of the implementation of Basel III, AOCI has been gradually integrated to regulatory capital for large banks (Fuster and Vickery (2018)). In practice, banks face a wide range of regulatory constraints—Greenwood et al. (2017) list at least 10, with different constraints binding for different banks. Some of those constraints, most clearly the capital charges implied by the Comprehensive Capital Analysis and Review (CCAR) and Dodd-Frank Act stress tests, are directly relaxed by a higher NIM through higher “preprovision net revenue,” which is consistent with the positive dependence ofϕ¯t$\overline{\phi }_{t}$in bank profitability. The 2023 banking turmoil highlighted the role of accounting rules (e.g., hold-to-maturity vs. available-for-sale assets) as well as the limitations of book value accounting, as unrealized losses still ended up recognized by markets (e.g., when Silicon Valley Bank tried to raise equity) and uninsured depositors.
SectionIII.Bfollows the empirical literature in using heterogeneity in deposit pass-through to test the model's mechanism in the cross section of banks. In the baseline model, banks are symmetric. To interpret the cross-sectional evidence, it is useful to extend the model to allow for heterogeneous banks. The simplest way to do so is to assume that heterogeneity arises from the demand side, for instance, if banks operate in segmented markets that differ in depositors' preferences over money and deposits. The following result shows the implications for deposit pass-through and other variables such as lending and loan spreads.
Proposition 5. (Heterogeneous deposit rate pass-through)Supposex$x$is CES (9) and banks operate in segmented markets that differ only in the preference parameters(α,χ)$(\alpha,\chi)$. Consider two marketsj=A,B$j=A,B$withαA>αB$\alpha _{A}>\alpha _{B}$andχA>χB$\chi _{A}>\chi _{B}$such that the initial deposit rates are the same, that is,sAd=sBd$s_{A}^{d}=s_{B}^{d}$andiAd=iBd$i_{A}^{d}=i_{B}^{d}$.
The long-run pass-through of the common nominal ratei$i$to the market-specific nominal deposit rateijd$i_{j}^{d}$is lower in marketA$A$:


Proposition5states that controlling for the initial deposit rate, the pass-through of the market ratei$i$to deposit rates is weaker in the market with a stronger relative preference for cash over deposits. Thus, the deposit rate beta (defined in Proposition3) is lower in marketA$A$. In SectionIII.B, I show empirically that, in response to a persistent decline in rates, banks with a lower interest expense beta (i.e., a measure of the deposit rate beta, as in marketA$A$here) experienced lower loan growth and a larger increase in loan spreads, which is consistent with Proposition5.
The higher parametersα$\alpha$andχ$\chi$can be viewed as capturing a lower level of financial sophistication in marketA$A$: households assign a higher value to cash and deposits,χA>χB$\chi _{A}>\chi _{B}$, for instance, because they can less easily transfer funds from high-yield accounts to transaction accounts, and put a higher weight on cash relative to deposits,αA>αB$\alpha _{A}>\alpha _{B}$, for instance, because they rely less on payment technologies and more on cash transactions. This is reflected in higher steady-state ratios of savers' liquid asset holdings to consumptioni·m+sddcs=χ$\frac{i\cdot m+s^{d}d}{c^{s}}=\chi$and of money to depositsmd=α1−αsdiε$\frac{m}{d}=\frac{\alpha }{1-\alpha }\left(\frac{s^{d}}{i}\right)^{\epsilon }$in marketA$A$.27
This result is consistent with the literature on deposit market power, in which deposit rate pass-through depends on both the demand side through depositors' preferences for money and deposits and the supply side through the degree of competition. In Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), deposit rate pass-through is lower when banks have more market power (proxied by a higher local deposit market concentration), but also when households are less financially sophisticated (proxied by demographic characteristics such as older and less educated households).
The current model focuses on the interactions between the deposit side and the lending side of commercial banks, and ignores other sources of borrowing such as corporate bonds for firms and loans from nonbanks for households. In Sarto and Wang (2023), we study theoretically and empirically the link between the secular decline in interest rates and the contemporaneous rise in nonbank lending (as documented in, e.g., Buchak et al. (2018) for the U.S. mortgage market). To sketch how banks and nonbanks might interact, recall that at low interest rates, banks lose much of their deposit funding advantage, and as we have seen, equilibrium loan spreads increase sharply in a world without nonbanks. Once we add nonbanks to the picture, these larger loan spreads spur the entry and growth of nonbank lenders, who do not rely on deposit funding and so do not suffer from lower rates on the liability side, but have a different lending technology and perhaps a comparative advantage in lending to some types of borrowers.28This endogenous response will mitigate the rise in the loan spread for these products (although there should still remain a net increase to trigger the nonbank response), and the negative credit supply effects coming from the bank side. However, the composition of aggregate lending may also be tilted toward the type of loans that nonbanks specialize in (such as standardized mortgages) and potentially away from information-intensive and/or unsecured lending, for instance, to small businesses.
The model's tractable structure allows for many extensions, described inInternet AppendixSectionIII. SectionIII.Ainvestigates the role of money and shows that under a different long-run monetary policy regime that holds real balancesm$m$fixed, the inflation target adjusts to keepi$i$fixed in response to real rate shocks, which suppresses the effect on lending. SectionIII.Cshows that the results hold when banks are allowed to issue equity at a sufficiently high cost. In SectionIII.D, I add operating costs of making loans to capture other components of banks' return on assets. This model generates more realistic loan spreads (that are nonzero even in the unconstrained lending regimei>i¯$i>\bar{i}$) without altering the results. SectionIII.Eanalyzes the implications of loan spreads when firms use bank loans to finance investment by bank-dependent firms instead of consumption.
article.III. Supporting Aggregate and Cross-Sect:
I document several facts that support the model's long-run predictions. At the aggregate level, the secular decline in bond rates has led to a shift in the composition of bank interest income: deposit spreads have shrunk while loan spreads have widened, roughly one for one as in the model, so that the loan-deposit spread has remained stable.
Turning to cross-sectional evidence, bank-level patterns are consistent with the model's Proposition5: banks whose deposit rates have been historically less responsive to interest rates experienced slower equity and lending growth in the recent low-rate environment, and their loan spreads have increased by more.
I begin by showing that the steady decline in interest rates over the past 20 years has shifted the composition of bank income.29There has been a rise in the maturity-adjusted loan spread between loans and bonds that mirrors a decline in the deposit spread between bonds and deposits. I use quarterly income and balance sheet data for all United States commercial banks from the Call Reports and the FDIC Quarterly Banking Profile.
Figure8(left panel) decomposes the total spread between loan interest income (as a fraction of total loans) and deposit interest expense (as a fraction of total deposits) for U.S. banks between 1997Q2 and 2018Q2. The corresponding total spread is the sum of the red and blue areas. It has been remarkably stable in spite of a large decline in interest rates. The right panel shows the model-implied paths of loan and deposit spreads in response to a shock to the path of bond pricesqt$q_{t}$taking place in 2000 that matches the realized rates in the U.S. from 2000 to 2018.
I focus on the post-1997Q2 period, for which the Call Reports contain detailed information on the repricing maturity structure of assets and liabilities needed to construct the loan spread, but the stability of the total loan-deposit spread goes back much further, in spite of wide variations in interest rates, as shown in FigureIA.5.
My findings are consistent with Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl's (2021) work on the stability of the NIM, which also includes interest income from securities and interest expense on wholesale liabilities. In fact, the loan-deposit spread has beenmore stablethan the NIM, which has fallen from 4.4% in 1997Q2 to a low of 2.95% in 2015Q1, before slightly rebounding to 3.2% in 2018. The reason is that the return on securities has fallen by more than the return on loans, consistent with my theory in which banks are the marginal pricers of loans but not of bonds. Hence, the NIM would have fallen by even more than it has had the loan spread not increased.
Duration is not the only difference between loans and bonds. In particular, higher loan spreads could be driven by higher credit risk and/or higher bank operating costs. While controlling for expected credit risk properly would require an asset pricing model in the spirit of Begenau, Piazzesi, and Schneider (2015), FigureIA.6shows that realized credit risk and operating costs are unlikely to explain the discrepancy either: realized loss provisions were high during the Great Recession but have reverted (since around 2012) to the same levels as in the 2000 to 2008 period, while operating costs, measured by noninterest expense, have fallen by around 1% of earning assets. Fees and other sources of noninterest income have also decreased.
Even holding credit risk constant, part of the higher excess loan spread that I find could be due to a higher price of credit risk. A risk-based explanation would imply a starker increase in the loan spread for riskier loans. FigureIA.7, however, shows the same pattern (using data from the Federal Reserve's Survey of Terms of Business Lending) as in Figure8for very short-term, low risk, commercial and industrial loans.32Moreover, the increase in credit spreads seems specific to bank loans. FigureIA.8shows the corporate bond spread constructed by Gilchrist and Zakrajsek (2012), extended through August 2016. Its 2010 to 2016 average equals 2.3%, almost exactly the same value as the 1997 to 2007 average of 2.2%. Therefore, the rise in loan spreads would be difficult to explain through a model of loan credit risk premia based on matching loans with corporate bonds, as in Begenau, Piazzesi, and Schneider (2015).
Finally, the pattern is unlikely to stem from an increase in the marginal cost of wholesale funding relative to Treasuries (or equivalently, a higher liquidity premium on Treasuries): the spread between the three-month LIBOR (the leading maturity) and three-month T-bills has averaged 36 bps between 2000 and 2006, and 29 bps between 2010 and 2019.
Still, simple correlations between interest rates and aggregate spreads could be driven by mechanisms besides those at play in the model, such as changes in liquidity or leverage regulation or evolution in the banking sector's market structure. Moreover, trends in aggregate quantities may be dominated by the “growth of finance,” as discussed in SectionII.A. I next turn to cross-sectional data to shed more light on the causal chain behind the rise in loan spreads.
Banks are heterogeneous in how they adjust deposit rates to interest rates. Variation in pass-through can come from the demand side (e.g., differences in preferences over cash and deposits) as in SectionII.Dand/or from the supply side (e.g., differences in concentration) as in the literature on deposit market power (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)). I now study how the long-run decline in interest rates has affected different banks depending on how sticky their deposit rates have been historically. This cross-sectional variation can help isolate the effect of the secular decline in interest rates from other contemporaneous aggregate shocks.
TableIIreports the results for all banks.35The estimates are positive and significant for retained earnings, equity, and loans, and negative and significant for the loan spread. This means that banks with a larger decrease in the liability spread also experienced lower retained earnings, slower equity and loan growth, and a larger increase in loan spreads. All these predictions are consistent with the mechanism of the model (in particular, Proposition5on cross-sectional predictions with heterogeneous deposit markets), which predicts that the decrease in the liability spread hurts retained earnings and thus equity growth (because it is not offset by higher equity issuance), which feeds into lower loan growth and higher loan spreads due to the leverage constraint tying lending to equity.
article.IV. Conclusion:
In this paper, I argue that due to the dual role of commercial banks as credit and liquidity providers, the secular decline in interest rates hurts long-run bank credit supply, even well above the ZLB. Because privately issued deposits compete with publicly issued money, a lower nominal rate compresses deposit spreads, which hurts bank profitability and lending. Lower rates can be expansionary for bank lending in the short run but contractionary in the long run. The model has new implications for the optimal inflation target, as policymakers must trade off minimizing the cost of liquidity (as under the standard Friedman rule) against the consequences in credit markets.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
